12: A direct revelation: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among people by which we must be saved.' The exclusivity claim (ouk estin en allō oudeni hē sōtēria) is made before the supreme Jewish court that had condemned Jesus. Peter's Christocentrism is absolute and public: not 'Jesus is one path among many' but the singular name by which salvation comes. The dei (must) is divine necessity — salvation through this name is not a human option but a divine appointment. The Christological uniqueness of Acts 4:12 is the evangelical confession that animates the entire mission of the book.
1: The Sadducees' opposition to the apostles is rooted in their denial of resurrection — but the resurrection of Jesus is precisely the claim Peter has just made (Acts 3:15, 26). Jesus had predicted this opposition: 'they will hand you over to the local councils' (Matthew 10:17); 'when they bring you before synagogues and rulers and authorities, do not be anxious about how you should defend yourself' (Luke 12:11). The arrest of the apostles for proclaiming resurrection in Jesus is the first fulfillment of the passion narrative's pattern extending into the community.
2: The apostles are teaching 'the resurrection of the dead in Jesus' (tēn anastasin tēn ek nekrōn en tō Iēsou) — not resurrection as a general doctrine but specifically through Jesus as the firstfruit case. This is the precise christological claim that enrages the Sadducees and defines the early mission: resurrection has happened, concretely, in a named person, and this person is the basis for universal resurrection hope. The pattern of religious authority opposition to resurrection proclamation fulfills Jesus's warnings in Luke 21:12–15.
3: The detention 'until the next day' because it was already evening reflects the same Sanhedrin procedural constraints that governed Jesus's trial — formal Jewish trials could not be conducted at night (m. Sanhedrin Acts 4:1). The pattern of arrest → overnight detention → trial the next morning mirrors the passion narrative's structure. Peter and John stand where Jesus stood.
4: Five thousand men believe despite the arrest — the multiplication of the community under opposition is the fulfillment of Jesus's parable of the sower (Luke 8:8, 'a hundredfold') and his prediction that 'the gates of hell shall not prevail' against the church (Matthew 16:18). Opposition from the authorities accelerates rather than stops the mission's growth — a pattern that recurs throughout Acts and that Jesus had explicitly predicted ('they will persecute you from city to city,' Matthew 23:34).
5: The gathering of 'rulers, elders, and scribes' in Jerusalem is the same body that condemned Jesus — Annas, Caiaphas, and the high priestly family appear by name in Acts 4:6. The apostles are arraigned before the identical tribunal. Luke's christological point is structural: what the Sanhedrin did to Jesus it is now doing to Jesus's witnesses, and the outcome will mirror the pattern — apparent defeat by institutional power, followed by divine vindication.
6: Annas (still treated as the real high priest despite formal deposition by Rome) and Caiaphas (his son-in-law, current high priest) are the same pair who presided over Jesus's trial (Luke 22:54; John 18:13, 24). Their reappearance creates explicit continuity between the trial of Jesus and the trial of his witnesses. The same hands that handed Jesus to Pilate now interrogate the witnesses to his resurrection.
7: 'By what power or by what name did you do this?' — the Sanhedrin's question about authority echoes their challenge to Jesus in Luke 20:2 ('By what authority do you do these things?'). Jesus had answered with a counter-question and a veiled claim to divine authority; Peter will answer directly with a name — the name they condemned. The reversal is total: what they meant to suppress by crucifixion has become the operative power in Jerusalem.
8: 'Filled with the Holy Spirit' — Peter's Spirit-empowered defense fulfills Jesus's promise exactly: 'When they bring you before synagogues and rulers and authorities, do not be anxious about how you should defend yourself or what you should say, for the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what you ought to say' (Luke 12:11–12; cf. Matthew 10:19–20). The fulfillment is precise: the moment of Sanhedrin interrogation triggers the promised Spirit-speech.
9: 'If we are being examined today concerning a good deed done to a crippled man' — Peter frames the interrogation as absurdity: the authorities are investigating a healing. The framing is rhetorically powerful and theologically pointed: the Sanhedrin which condemned the one who healed on the Sabbath (Luke 6:6–11; Acts 13:10–17) is now interrogating those who healed in his name. The pattern of authorities opposing the good that Jesus and his agents do is the fulfillment of Jesus's indictment: 'Woe to you lawyers, for you have taken away the key of knowledge; you did not enter yourselves, and you hindered those who were entering' (Luke 11:52).
10: 'By the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead — by him this man is standing before you well.' The direct naming of the Sanhedrin's crime — 'whom you crucified' — echoes Peter's Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:23, 36) and is the christological center of the defense. The contrast 'you crucified / God raised' is the gospel's essential polarity: human rejection and divine vindication. The healed man standing is the visible evidence of resurrection power operative in Jesus's name.
11: 'This Jesus is the stone that was rejected by you, the builders, which has become the cornerstone.' Peter cites Psalms 118:22 — the same text Jesus cited in the temple (Luke 20:17: the stone the builders rejected becoming the cornerstone). Jesus had applied it to himself and to his rejection by the chief priests; Peter now applies it directly to the crucifixion and resurrection, with the Sanhedrin as the builders and Jesus as the rejected-then-vindicated stone. The christological argument is complete: the scripture predicted this rejection, the rejection has occurred, and the resurrection confirms the scripture's fulfillment.
13: 'Unlettered and ordinary men' (agrammatos kai idiōtai) — the Sanhedrin's assessment that Peter and John had no formal scribal training (no studied at the feet of a recognized rabbi) makes their eloquence inexplicable without the Spirit. 'They recognized that they had been with Jesus' — the perceived connection is not formal discipleship but the distinctive presence of one who walked with Jesus. The fulfilled word: 'A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone when he is fully trained will be like his teacher' (Luke 6:40). Being with Jesus has transferred something to the disciples that scribal training alone cannot explain.
14: The healed man standing with Peter and John renders the Sanhedrin's situation impossible — they cannot deny the sign without looking absurd before the city. The man's presence as a walking proof defeats the institutional power's ability to suppress the truth. Jesus had said, 'If these were silent, the very stones would cry out' (Luke 19:40) — and here, in a sense, a man who was formerly immobile is the irrefutable witness.
15: The Sanhedrin's private deliberation mirrors their private deliberations about Jesus (Luke 22:66–71; Mark 14:55–64) — the judicial body again trying to manage a situation they cannot control. Their problem now is not Jesus (whom they killed) but the impossible persistence of his effects: a healed man, Spirit-filled disciples, and a growing community. The resurrection has made their solution to the Jesus problem worse than the problem itself.
16: 'We cannot deny it' — the Sanhedrin's internal confession of impotence is Luke's ironic climax. The ruling body of Israel acknowledges, in private, that they cannot suppress the evidence of Jesus's power. The institution that condemned Jesus as a false messiah who could not save himself now confesses it cannot stop what his name produces. The public suppression they choose (Acts 4:17) in the face of private acknowledgment is the continuation of the bad faith that characterized the Passion.
17: 'That it may spread no further' — the Sanhedrin's agenda is containment: the same word (diaphtheirō, diaskorpizō) Jesus used for spreading. Their command mirrors Caiaphas's pragmatic judgment at Jesus's trial: 'It is better that one man die for the people' (John 11:49–50) — institutional management of theological danger through suppression. But Jesus had predicted the outcome: 'What you have whispered in private rooms shall be proclaimed on the housetops' (Luke 12:3).
18: 'Not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus' — the explicit prohibition is the empire of human authority attempting to silence the name. Jesus had said, 'Everyone who acknowledges me before men, I also will acknowledge before my Father' (Matthew 10:32); the apostles' refusal to comply with this prohibition is the lived enactment of this acknowledgment. The prohibition creates the conditions for the confession of Acts 4:19–20.
19: 'Whether it is right in God's sight to listen to you rather than to God, you must judge' — Peter and John's response is the foundational statement of Christian civil disobedience: where human authority conflicts with divine command, divine authority supersedes. The formulation echoes Socrates' defense in the Apology (Plato, Apol. 29d: 'I shall obey God rather than you') — a point Luke's educated audience would recognize. But the christological grounding is different: Peter and John obey God because God has raised Jesus and commanded proclamation in his name.
20: 'We cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard' — eyewitness obligation as the grounds for disobedience. The 'we have seen and heard' is the epistemic foundation of apostolic witness (cf. 1 John 1:1–3; 2 Peter 1:16). Jesus had said, 'You are witnesses of these things' (Luke 24:48) — the witness commission overrides the Sanhedrin's suppression command because it comes from a higher authority.
21: The release without punishment — because the city glorifies God for the healing — mirrors the partial protection Jesus enjoyed from the crowds during his Jerusalem ministry (Luke 19:47–48; Acts 22:2). The people function as a check on the Sanhedrin's power, preventing official violence where the evidence of divine action is too public to suppress. The risen Lord's community operates under the same dynamic that protected Jesus himself: popular recognition of divine power constrains official hostility.
22: The man was over forty years old — a detail that emphasizes the duration and certainty of his disability (congenital lame from birth, Acts 3:2, now over forty). No natural explanation could account for recovery from this condition. The age detail is Luke the physician's marker: this is medically impossible, which makes the theological claim unavoidable.
23: The apostles' return to 'their own people' — the community of disciples — and their report of the Sanhedrin's words triggers a corporate prayer that is one of the most christologically dense passages in Acts. The community's response to institutional opposition is not political maneuvering but prayer grounded in scripture, specifically Psalm 2. The gathering mirrors Jesus's own post-temptation return to prayer and community.
24: 'Sovereign Lord, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and everything in them' — the prayer opens with God as creator and sovereign (despota), a title that frames the Sanhedrin's authority as derivative and limited. The God who made everything is not threatened by the rulers of a small province; the prayer's opening establishes the perspective from which all that follows is interpreted. The community prays from the vantage point of divine sovereignty, not human anxiety.
25: 'Who through the mouth of our father David, your servant, said by the Holy Spirit: Why did the Gentiles rage, and the peoples plot in vain?' — Psalms 2:1–2 is cited as directly prophetic of the current moment. The attribution — through David, by the Holy Spirit — makes David the instrument and the Spirit the author, consistent with early Christian pneumatology of scripture (cf. 2 Peter 1:21). The 'nations raging' against the Lord's anointed is being fulfilled now, in Jerusalem, against Jesus and his witnesses.
26: 'The kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers were gathered together, against the Lord and against his Anointed.' The prayer identifies Herod and Pilate as the 'kings and rulers' of Psalm 2 — Gentile and Jewish power collaborating against Jesus, God's Anointed (christos). The interpretive move is bold: a psalm about universal Gentile opposition to God's king is applied to the specific historical alliance of Rome and Jerusalem against Jesus. The cosmic frame makes Pilate and Herod actors in a drama written before their birth.
27: 'Herod and Pontius Pilate, along with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel' — the specific historical agents of Jesus's crucifixion are named as the fulfillment of Psalm 2. The four-fold listing (Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, Israel) is comprehensive: all human power structures participated. This is not anti-Semitic targeting of Israel alone but a universal indictment — every human power structure aligned against God's anointed. The prayer names it clearly to frame the present opposition (Sanhedrin) as a continuation of the same alliance.
28: 'To do whatever your hand and your plan had predestined to take place' — the crucifixion was not a defeat but a divine plan fulfilled. God's prothesis (foreordained purpose) governed the actions of even those who opposed it: the same omnipotent sovereignty that created heaven and earth (Acts 4:24) also ordained that the Anointed's rejection would accomplish the plan of redemption. The community's confidence in the face of opposition is grounded in this recognition: opposition itself is within God's providential scope.
29: 'And now, Lord, look upon their threats and grant to your servants to continue to speak your word with all boldness' — the prayer does not ask for removal of opposition but for courage within it. Jesus had prayed for his disciples in the same posture: 'I do not ask that you take them out of the world, but that you keep them from the evil one' (John 17:15). The request for parrēsia (boldness/freedom of speech) is specifically the Spirit-given courage Jesus promised for Sanhedrin appearances (Luke 12:11–12).
30: 'While you stretch out your hand to heal, and signs and wonders are performed through the name of your holy servant Jesus' — the prayer requests continued healing signs in Jesus's name. 'Holy servant' (ton hagion paida sou) echoes the Isaiah 42–53 Servant songs; the same title was used in Acts 3:13, 26. The community prays for the continuation of the healing ministry Jesus modeled through his earthly life (Luke 4:18–19) now mediated through his risen name.
31: 'The place was shaken, and they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and continued to speak the word of God with boldness.' The immediate Spirit-filling and shaking of the place is a mini-Pentecost — the Spirit fulfills Jesus's promise in real time in response to prayer. The pattern: prayer → Spirit-filling → boldness → proclamation mirrors Acts 2 (prayer, Acts 1:14 → Spirit, Acts 2:4 → bold proclamation, Acts 2:14). The Spirit does not come once but repeatedly as the community returns to prayer.
32: 'The full number of those who believed were of one heart and soul, and no one said that any of the things that belonged to him was his own, but they had everything in common.' The description of the early community's unity and sharing echoes the idealized vision of Israel's covenant community — the Deuteronomic hope that there be 'no poor among you' (Deuteronomy 15:4). Jesus had called his disciples away from possessiveness: 'sell your possessions and give to the needy' (Luke 12:33); 'any one of you who does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple' (Luke 14:33). The community is embodying the Sermon's ethics corporately.
33: 'With great power the apostles were giving their testimony to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus, and great grace was upon them all.' The resurrection testimony and the common life are inseparably linked: the community's generosity is the visible evidence of a people who believe death has been defeated. If Jesus is risen, possessions have a different status; if death is conquered, the hoarding impulse — fundamentally a death-anxiety response — is transformed. The 'great grace' (charis megalē) on the community is the overflow of the grace that raised Jesus.
34: 'There was not a needy person among them' — the fulfillment of Deuteronomy 15:4 LXX: 'there shall be no poor person among you.' The Deuteronomic promise was conditional on Israel's full obedience to the covenant; the early Christian community, constituted around the risen Lord and filled with the Spirit, fulfills it organically rather than by legal compliance. Jesus had promised: 'seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you' (Matthew 6:33); the community's provision for the poor is the kingdom's concrete manifestation.
35: 'Distribution was made to each as any had need' — the principle of distribution according to need rather than status or merit reflects the kingdom's inversions. Jesus's feeding miracles (Luke 9:12–17) had demonstrated this principle physically: everyone receives what they need. The apostles' mediating role in distribution mirrors Jesus's role in the feeding miracles where the disciples serve as intermediaries of divine provision.
36: Joseph Barnabas — 'son of encouragement' (huios paraklēseōs) — is introduced precisely because his generosity will contrast with the Ananias-Sapphira deception in the next chapter. The naming of a specific generous giver before the narrative of false generosity serves to distinguish genuine from counterfeit covenant fidelity. Barnabas is a Levite of Cypriot origin — unexpected generosity from an unexpected quarter, consistent with Jesus's reversals of expectation about who embodies covenant faithfulness.
37: Barnabas sells a field and brings the full proceeds. His act establishes the communal norm that Ananias will violate — the contrast is deliberate. The unreserved giving of Barnabas reflects the spirit of Jesus's call: 'sell all that you have and distribute to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven, and come, follow me' (Luke 18:22). Whether the early community expected all property to be sold is debated, but Barnabas models the total-surrender posture Jesus modeled.
